results.phrase: We can't go out without setting the foundation. It needs more time.
results.usage: Use this when a launch deadline is approaching but the product is not actually ready to be seen.
results.topic: we're supposed to launch next week but the product still has too many rough edges

results.phrase: We keep changing the part before the hair dries.
results.usage: Use this when leadership is resetting direction before any previous decision has had time to take effect.
results.topic: leadership keeps changing the priorities mid-sprint and the team is frustrated
results.note: You can change your part nothing happens - maybe while styling or blow drying

results.phrase: We've been on our feet in heels for two months. It's time to sit down.
results.usage: Use this when a team has been pushing hard long enough that rest is no longer optional.
results.topic: the team has been in crunch mode for two months and morale is tanking
results.note: Nothing about standing, chair, makeup bags, brushes nothing structural it literally doesn’t make any sense

results.phrase: We need a deep conditioning treatment, not another serum.
results.usage: Use this when a team that has been pushed hard needs real rest and recovery, not another productivity fix layered on top.
results.topic: the team has been in crunch mode for two months and morale is tanking

results.phrase: We've been blending this out for three weeks. At some point you have to set it and move on.
results.usage: Use this when a team has been endlessly refining or debating something that needs to be locked in and acted on.
results.topic: we've been debating the same decision for three weeks and nothing is moving

results.phrase: We've been doing a no-makeup makeup look when the brief clearly called for a full glam.
results.usage: Use this when feedback signals that the team has been playing it too safe and needs to show up with more ambition and scale.
results.topic: we got feedback that our roadmap is too conservative and we need to take bigger swings
results.note: Use soft glam for no make up look always. It’s better. And then you have to change full glam. So this should be soft glam when you want a full beat

results.phrase: We've got two architects and neither one is willing to be the base coat.
results.usage: Use this when two senior people are each waiting for the other to yield, and the standoff is blocking everyone else from moving forward.
results.topic: two senior engineers fundamentally disagree on the technical approach and it's blocking the whole team
results.note: All of this sucks. No makeup brushes no chairs no brushes , no standing terms or anything structural. This makes literally no sense like who is the architect here what is the relevance of base coat? Conceptually none of these metaphors make sense they are not true. They are fake and made up and really not worth using over just regular English. Things need to be concise clear and catchy. That’s what an idiom or saying or phrase does.

results.phrase: We have to decide what goes in the bag and what stays on the counter.
results.usage: Use this when a team needs to make hard cuts but keeps avoiding the call on what actually makes it out the door.
results.topic: we have to cut features before launch but we can't agree on which ones to cut

results.phrase: We can't set the look if someone keeps handing us a different reference photo mid-application.
results.usage: Use this when leadership is changing direction mid-execution and the team needs that named clearly before the next sprint begins.
results.topic: leadership keeps changing the priorities mid-sprint and the team is frustrated
results.note: Why is this so fucking intense with a FORCED analogy

results.phrase: We're not getting more brushes. We have to make the ones we have work.
